data_flow_trace.original: Genesis.initialize calls Reconfiguration.initialize() in step 6 BEFORE Blocker.initialize (step 7)
data_flow_trace.current_state_summary: Genesis.initialize calls Reconfiguration(SystemAddresses.RECONFIGURATION).initialize() at line 167 and Blocker(SystemAddresses.BLOCK).initialize() at line 170; order is Reconfiguration first, then Blocker.
data_flow_trace.gating_conditions: Only SYSTEM_CALLER can invoke Genesis.initialize (Genesis.sol:145) and this executes once per chain lifecycle.

data_flow_trace.original: Reconfiguration.initialize reads ITimestamp.nowMicroseconds() at Reconfiguration.sol:69; Timestamp has no initializer and Blocker has not run, so the read returns 0
data_flow_trace.current_state_summary: initialize() body on line 69 sets currentEpoch = 1 and line 70 sets lastReconfigurationTime = ITimestamp(SystemAddresses.TIMESTAMP).nowMicroseconds(). Timestamp.sol declares `uint64 public microseconds` (src/runtime/Timestamp.sol:29) with no initializer function and nowMicroseconds() just returns the storage slot (src/runtime/Timestamp.sol:47-49). Because Blocker.initialize() has not yet executed, the returned value is the default 0.
data_flow_trace.gating_conditions: Timestamp exposes no initializer; its microseconds storage is zero until Blocker.initialize() or onBlockStart writes it. At the call site inside Genesis, neither has happened yet.

data_flow_trace.original: lastReconfigurationTime is persisted as 0 for the entire post-genesis period
data_flow_trace.current_state_summary: lastReconfigurationTime is a uint64 storage slot (line 46) written only by initialize (line 70) and _applyReconfiguration (line 293). Since the initialize call captured 0, the value remains 0 until the first epoch transition completes.
data_flow_trace.gating_conditions: No direct setter exists; Blocker.initialize writes timestamp to 0 again (src/blocker/Blocker.sol:66) so even after Genesis completes lastReconfigurationTime==0 and microseconds==0.

data_flow_trace.original: Block 1 triggers Blocker.onBlockStart -> Timestamp.updateGlobalTime writes real current time
data_flow_trace.current_state_summary: onBlockStart calls updateGlobalTime(validatorAddress, timestampMicros) at line 107, then IReconfiguration(...).checkAndStartTransition() at line 112. For a non-NIL proposer the Timestamp contract requires timestamp > current (src/runtime/Timestamp.sol:83-87) and writes the new real timestamp.
data_flow_trace.gating_conditions: proposerIndex != NIL_PROPOSER_INDEX (i.e., a real validator produced the block). If consensus only emits NIL blocks, time stays at 0; but any real first block satisfies this.

data_flow_trace.original: Reconfiguration.checkAndStartTransition evaluates (currentTime - 0) >> epochInterval — trivially true — an epoch transition and DKG round start on block 1 before any warm-up window
data_flow_trace.current_state_summary: _canTransition (lines 215-219) returns currentTime >= lastReconfigurationTime + epochInterval. With lastReconfigurationTime==0 and realistic wall-clock timestamp (~1.8e15 microseconds), the check is trivially true and checkAndStartTransition proceeds through evictUnderperformingValidators and _startDkgSession / _doImmediateReconfigure on the first real block.
data_flow_trace.gating_conditions: No prior DkgInProgress state (fresh genesis guarantees Idle). EpochConfig.epochIntervalMicros returns the configured interval (e.g., 1 hour) which is far below the wall-clock value so the invariant is trivially violated.

reasoning_chain: Genesis.sol:167 invokes Reconfiguration.initialize before Blocker.initialize (line 170). Reconfiguration.initialize line 70 unconditionally reads ITimestamp.nowMicroseconds() and stores it into lastReconfigurationTime. Timestamp.sol exposes no initializer and its storage slot microseconds defaults to 0; no writer has been called before this point in Genesis. Therefore lastReconfigurationTime is persistently 0. Blocker.initialize (Blocker.sol:66) then writes timestamp=0 again as a NIL block placeholder. When the first real-proposer block arrives, Blocker.onBlockStart (lines 107-112) first calls Timestamp.updateGlobalTime with the real timestamp (which must strictly advance past 0 — trivially true) and then calls Reconfiguration.checkAndStartTransition. _canTransition evaluates currentTime >= 0 + epochInterval, which is trivially satisfied by any realistic wall-clock value, causing an immediate eviction sweep and DKG start (or immediate reconfigure when RandomnessConfig variant is Off) on block 1. No guard in Reconfiguration, Blocker, or Timestamp prevents the sequence. The integration test ConsensusEngineFlow.t.sol:170-176 even pre-sets Timestamp to INITIAL_TIME before reconfig.initialize with the comment 'so lastReconfigurationTime is set correctly', confirming awareness but not a fix. The Genesis flow itself performs no such pre-seeding. The epochInterval guarantee for the first (genesis) epoch is silently degraded.

key_evidence.why_matters: Confirms Reconfiguration.initialize runs before any writer touches the Timestamp contract in the production genesis path.
The state-of-record for the reconfiguration deadline is captured from an uninitialized timestamp.
Timestamp has no initializer; the storage slot is 0 until a writer (Blocker) sets it, so the read in Reconfiguration.initialize returns 0.
Even after Blocker.initialize executes (genesis step 7), the timestamp is still 0; re-reading now would not help, and Reconfiguration.initialize has already frozen lastReconfigurationTime at 0.
Transition guard compares against lastReconfigurationTime; with that value being 0 the predicate is trivially true on the first real block.
Shows the attack trigger: first non-NIL block advances Timestamp to real wall-clock value and then immediately invokes checkAndStartTransition on the same block.
Test setup manually pre-seeds the timestamp in a way that Genesis.sol does NOT, masking the issue in integration tests but confirming the ordering hazard is recognized.

counterargument: One could argue that in practice the consensus engine emits only NIL blocks (proposer == SYSTEM_CALLER) during the warm-up phase, and the NIL branch in Timestamp.updateGlobalTime (src/runtime/Timestamp.sol:75-80) forbids the timestamp from changing, so microseconds stays at 0 until all validators are stably online. In that world _canTransition would keep returning false (currentTime 0 < 0 + epochInterval). However, nothing in the Solidity contracts enforces this behavior; Blocker.onBlockStart accepts any validator index and any timestamp > 0 on the very first real block. The docstring at Reconfiguration.sol:25-29 and the epochInterval semantics are the only documented guarantee and they are silently voided as soon as any validator proposes. Even assuming NIL-only warm-up, the first genuine block from a validator — which is the normal success path — triggers the transition, defeating the buffer. The moment wall-clock time is injected, the comparison becomes trivially true because the left-hand side is of order 1e15 microseconds while lastReconfigurationTime remains 0.
related_existing_tests: test/unit/blocker/Reconfiguration.t.sol:274-289 (test_initialize_success — manually calls Timestamp.updateGlobalTime to INITIAL_TIME before asserting lastReconfigurationTime==INITIAL_TIME, thereby hiding the 0-timestamp race)

test/unit/integration/ConsensusEngineFlow.t.sol:170-176 (setUp pre-seeds Timestamp before reconfig.initialize with explicit comment 'so lastReconfigurationTime is set correctly' — no test exercises the natural Genesis ordering)
test/unit/GenesisTest.t.sol:61-172 (test_Genesis_Success — does not assert lastReconfigurationTime or simulate the first post-genesis block, so the race is not covered)
